nativeRange.habitat: Shallow tropical reefs, seagrass beds, and soft muddy or sandy substrates near coastal areas; often found hiding inside empty bivalve shells, gastropod shells, and reef rubble.

decorPreferences: ample live rock with caves and crevices
anchored or glued rockwork (octopuses are surprisingly strong)
empty bivalve and gastropod shells as dens
dim lighting, ideally low actinic only
absolutely escape-proof lid with every opening sealed (cable ports, overflow boxes, return-pump gaps)

diet.notes: In the wild, the Dwarf Octopus feeds on small crustaceans, bivalves, and small fish. In captivity it generally accepts live feeder shrimp — saltwater species are preferred over freshwater ghost shrimp — along with small live or fresh-frozen crabs and pieces of raw shrimp or scallop offered from a feeding stick. Juveniles often require live prey before they can be coaxed onto thawed or frozen foods. Because the species is sensitive to elevated nitrates and phosphates, any uneaten food should be removed from the tank promptly.

avoidWith: all fish (will be stressed and stalked, or eat the octopus's food)
any tankmate added to a copper-treated system

summary: The Dwarf Octopus is a small tropical cephalopod from the Caribbean and Gulf of Mexico, generally regarded as suitable for expert keepers only. It requires a fully escape-proof, species-only aquarium and has a captive lifespan of roughly six to twelve months.
careNotes: Dwarf Octopus is a trade name that covers two very similar Caribbean cephalopods: Octopus joubini (the Atlantic Pygmy Octopus, which produces small eggs) and Octopus mercatoris (the Caribbean Pygmy Octopus, which produces larger eggs). The two are frequently sold interchangeably under the joubini name. Both species occur from Florida through the Gulf of Mexico and the wider Caribbean, typically from the surface down to 15 m depth on soft substrates and reef rubble, and both share essentially the same husbandry requirements. Adult mantle length is generally around 4-5 cm with arms reaching 7-9 cm, giving a total spread of roughly 10-13 cm including arms. Lighting should be kept dim — bright reef LEDs tend to stress octopuses, and dim actinic or low-blue lighting is preferred. The species is sensitive to elevated nitrates, phosphates, and any copper-based medication, so the tank should be copper-free for its entire history and run at conservatively low nutrient levels. Two requirements are non-negotiable for keeping any octopus: first, a species-only tank — fish tend to pester the octopus during feeding, and large invertebrates (shrimp, crabs, urchins, hermit crabs) will be treated as prey; second, a fully sealed lid — octopuses are capable of squeezing through any gap larger than their beak, and escaping the tank is among the most commonly reported causes of death for captive specimens. Every cable port, overflow box opening, and return-pump gap must be sealed. Juveniles often need live prey (saltwater feeder shrimp, amphipods) before they can be transitioned onto frozen shrimp or scallop offered from a feeding stick. Approach the tank slowly to avoid triggering an ink discharge, which can rapidly crash water quality in a small system and requires an immediate large water change. The blue-ringed octopuses (genus Hapalochlaena) are not related to this entry — their bite is considered invariably lethal and there is no antivenom, so they should never be kept in a home aquarium under any circumstances.
breedingNotes: Females lay 140 to 2,500+ eggs — smaller in O. joubini, larger in O. mercatoris — inside an empty shell or cave and brood them without feeding until they hatch. Like all octopuses, both the female and male die shortly after reproduction; this once-in-a-lifetime breeding strategy, known as semelparity, is normal for the genus. The small-egged O. joubini produces planktonic paralarvae (free-swimming larvae that drift in the water column) that are extremely difficult to rear in captivity. O. mercatoris, by contrast, hatches as miniature benthic octopuses — tiny, bottom-dwelling versions of the adult — that can be raised with sustained live-food culture, and this species is the form most often bred successfully by dedicated cephalopod keepers. Either way, breeding signals the end of the parent's life, so captive reproduction does not sustain a long-term population in a home aquarium.

additional.notes: Cross-checked tank size (30 gal for a pygmy), species-only requirement, escape-proofing, feeding-stick technique, ~6 month lifespan for dwarf species, and the joubini/mercatoris pygmy distinction.

additional.name: Wikipedia - Atlantic pygmy octopus
additional.notes: Confirmed taxonomy (Octopodidae, Octopoda), mantle length 4.5 cm + arms 9 cm, wild temperature 18-25 °C, depth 10-15 m, lifespan 6-12 months temperature-dependent, and the brood-size difference distinguishing joubini (small-egg, planktonic) from mercatoris (large-egg, benthic hatchlings).

hobbyistAdvisory.reason: specialist tank required: escape-proof lid, species-only, short captive lifespan, live prey for juveniles
